question: What actions should be taken when two power-driven vessels are meeting head-on?
ideal_answer: Under Rule 14, when two power-driven vessels are meeting on reciprocal or nearly reciprocal courses so as to involve risk of collision, each shall alter her course to starboard so that each passes on the port side of the other. This situation exists when a vessel sees the other ahead or nearly ahead, and by night she could see the masthead lights in a line or nearly in a line and both sidelights. When in doubt, she shall assume such a situation exists and act accordingly.
key_points: Rule 14 applies — head-on situation
Each vessel shall alter course to starboard
Pass port to port
By night: both masthead lights in line and both sidelights visible
When in doubt, assume head-on
unacceptable_answers: Suggesting one vessel has priority over the other

examiner_preferences.style_notes: Examiner wants practical understanding, not flashcard recitation. Ask what they would actually do on the bridge.

follow_ups: What if the other vessel doesn't alter?
At what angle does a head-on situation become a crossing situation?
What lights would you see at night to identify this as head-on?
common_mistakes: Not mentioning the doubt clause in Rule 14(c)
Forgetting to mention the visual cues for identifying head-on
Not knowing when head-on becomes crossing
examiner_notes: Candidates should reference Rule 14 specifically. Common mistake: forgetting that this rule applies when vessels are in sight of one another.

question: A power-driven vessel is crossing from your starboard side. What are your obligations?
ideal_answer: Under Rule 15, when two power-driven vessels are crossing so as to involve risk of collision, the vessel which has the other on her starboard side shall keep out of the way. As the give-way vessel under Rule 16, I must take early and substantial action to keep well clear. I should avoid crossing ahead of the other vessel. Practically, I would alter course to starboard to pass under her stern, or reduce speed, or both. The key is making my action early enough and obvious enough that the stand-on vessel can see my intentions on radar.
key_points: Rule 15 — crossing situation
You are the give-way vessel when the other is on your starboard side
Avoid crossing ahead of the other vessel
Rule 16 — take early and substantial action
Alter to starboard to pass under stern, or reduce speed
unacceptable_answers: Small incremental course changes instead of substantial action

examiner_preferences.style_notes: Wants to hear what you would physically do, not just what the rule says.

follow_ups: What should the stand-on vessel do if the give-way vessel fails to act?
When does Rule 17(b) permit the stand-on vessel to take action?
How would you determine risk of collision exists?
common_mistakes: Not explaining the practical action they would take
Forgetting Rule 16 requirement for early and substantial action
examiner_notes: Ensure candidates understand the starboard-side priority rule. They should explain the difference between give-way and stand-on obligations clearly.

question: I'm showing you a vessel on the slideshow. She's displaying two red lights in a vertical line. No other lights. What is she and what are your actions?
ideal_answer: That is a vessel not under command, showing two all-round red lights in a vertical line as required by Rule 27(a). She is not making way as there are no sidelights or sternlight showing. Under Rule 18, as a power-driven vessel I shall keep out of her way. I would assess the situation using radar and visual bearings, determine if risk of collision exists, and if so alter my course to starboard to pass well clear, giving her a wide berth as she cannot manoeuvre effectively.
key_points: Vessel not under command — Rule 27(a)
Two all-round red lights vertical line
Not making way — no sidelights or sternlight
Rule 18 — power-driven vessel keeps clear
Give wide berth as she cannot manoeuvre
unacceptable_answers: Not recognising absence of sidelights means not making way

examiner_preferences.style_notes: Start with identification, then explain what you see, then your actions. Examiner wants practical response not textbook.

follow_ups: What would change if she also showed sidelights and a sternlight?
What day shape would she show?
Give me the definition of not under command from the COLREGs
common_mistakes: Not noting the significance of missing sidelights
Giving flashcard answer without practical actions
examiner_notes: From real exam report: examiner showed slides of vessel light configurations and wanted definitions plus practical actions. Did not want flashcard answers.

question: I'm showing you a radar screen. We're in restricted visibility. Talk me through Rule 19 and what you would do with these targets.
ideal_answer: Rule 19 applies to vessels not in sight of one another in restricted visibility. Every vessel must proceed at a safe speed adapted for the conditions. If I detect another vessel by radar alone and risk of collision is developing, I must take avoiding action in ample time. If I need to alter course, I should avoid altering to port for a vessel forward of my beam, and avoid altering towards a vessel abeam or abaft the beam. I would use the radar plotting facilities — either manual or ARPA — to determine the target's course, speed, CPA and TCPA. I'd plot the WAO triangle to determine the true vector. If the CPA is too close, I would take early action and use the trial manoeuvre function to check my proposed alteration before executing it.
key_points: Rule 19 — restricted visibility, not in sight
Safe speed for conditions
Avoid altering to port for vessel forward of beam
Avoid altering towards vessel abeam or abaft beam
Use radar plotting — CPA, TCPA, true vectors
WAO triangle for manual plotting
Trial manoeuvre function to verify action
unacceptable_answers: Applying Rules 12-18 (these only apply in sight)
Not knowing what WAO triangle is

examiner_preferences.style_notes: Examiner showed radar screen and expected candidate to talk through the full process including plotting. Wanted to see practical radar work, not just rule recitation.

follow_ups: Show me the WAO triangle on this radar plot
What else can we use on the radar to avoid collision?
Talk me through how you would use trial mode
common_mistakes: Not knowing the difference between Rules 12-18 and Rule 19 application
Unable to explain radar plotting practically
examiner_notes: From real exam: examiner showed radar screen with two targets (port forward and abeam starboard). Wanted full Rule 19 application with practical radar plotting including WAO triangle and trial manoeuvre. This is a high-value question.

question: I'm showing you some buoyage. Identify this mark and tell me what your actions would be.
ideal_answer: That is a starboard lateral mark under IALA Region A. It is a green conical buoy. It marks the starboard side of a channel when proceeding in the conventional direction of buoyage. I would leave this mark to starboard when entering harbour or heading with the flood tide. At night it shows a green flashing light.
key_points: Correct identification of mark type
IALA Region A or B — know the difference
Correct colour and shape
Correct action — which side to leave it
Night-time light characteristics
unacceptable_answers: Not knowing the conventional direction of buoyage

examiner_preferences.style_notes: Examiner showed multiple buoys in sequence: starboard lateral IALA A, north cardinal, east cardinal, west cardinal, emergency wreck, preferred channel marks IALA A & B. Rapid identification expected.

follow_ups: What about IALA Region B — how would this differ?
South cardinal — you're steering 270, what are your actions?
What is a preferred channel mark and how do you identify it?
common_mistakes: Confusing IALA A and B lateral colours
Not knowing cardinal mark top marks
visual_instruction: Show this buoy image. Do not describe it — candidate must identify it.
examiner_notes: From real exam: examiner showed multiple buoys in rapid succession. Wanted identification, meaning, and actions for each. This is a core visual identification skill.
